data.title: The Commerce Dept. will reschedule Q3 GDP, October income/outlays reports
data.description:
The Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis on Thursday said it will reschedule the releases of its Q3 GDP revision, Q3 corporate profits, and October Personal Income and Outlays reports, but didn't say when they'll be released.

It's notable that the agency rescheduled the data rather than canceled them. On Wednesday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics canceled its October jobs report and its September Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, because it couldn't retroactively collect the data.

The BEA's second estimate of Q3 GDP and preliminary corporate profits were originally scheduled for Nov. 26, the same day as it had expected to issue the October Personal Income and Outlays report. The BEA economic release schedule will continue to be updated as information becomes available, it said.

The personal income and outlays data are particularly important because they include the personal consumption expenditure price index, which the Federal Reserve relies on to gauge the inflation rate, especially the core PCE price index, which excludes food and energy.

The Federal Open Market Committee, which determines the Fed's policy rate, next meets on Dec. 9-10.

The BLS has scheduled the November Employment Situation report for Dec. 16, 2025, after the Fed meeting, and the October JOLTS for Dec. 9.

data.title: Nvidia's Q3 results and outlook see largely bullish views from analysts
data.description:
Shares of Nvidia (NVDA) dipped about 1% on Thursday despite an "exceptional" quarter and "stellar" guidance.

The stock gave up its gains of about 5% premarket, which would have seen the company's market cap set to get a boost of over $200B.

"Nvidia Corporation is upgraded to a "cautious Buy" after strong Q3 earnings and stellar Q4 guidance reinforce its growth momentum," said Seeking Alpha analyst Kenio Fontes.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bashar Issa noted that Nvidia delivered "exceptional revenue growth last quarter, fueled by robust demand from generative AI labs and hyperscalers." The analysts maintained his Buy rating on the stock, and said that the company trades at a premium valuation, justified by market leadership, but faces risks if graphics processing unit, or GPU, demand slows or saturation occurs. 

Nvidia's Data Center segment drove 66% year-over-year growth, with robust demand from the U.S. and European clients offsetting lost China revenue due to export restrictions, said Seeking Alpha analyst Agar Capital.

"Despite concerns about valuation and an AI bubble, NVDA's earnings growth outpaces its stock price, resulting in a lower forward P/E and strong justification for a continued rally," the analyst added. Agar Capital kept its Buy rating on the stock.

For the period ending Oct. 26, the Jensen Huang-led company said it earned $1.30 per share as revenue soared 62% year-over-year to come in at $57.01B. Both top and bottom-line numbers beat estimates. Looking to the fourth-quarter of fiscal 2026, Nvidia expects to generate $65B in revenue, plus or minus 2%. Analysts were forecasting $61.98B in revenue.

"Nvidia reported another exceptional quarter with revenues of $57 billion beating estimates by $1.9 billion. The AI GPU company even guided up FQ4 revenues to $65 billion with 75% gross margins, reinforcing the strong AI data center growth and earnings trends," said Seeking Alpha analyst Stone Fox Capital, Investing Group Leader for Out Fox The Street. "The stock isn’t likely to see a huge rally due to these strong earnings only adding to fears of hyperscaler overspending, but the stock is cheap with growth rates topping 50% while the stock trades at only roughly 27x FY27 EPS targets."

The U.S. tech giant's results and guidance drove semiconductor stocks higher premarket on Thursday. Year-to-date Nvidia's stock has surged about 39%. The company has a market cap (intraday) of about $4.537T as of Nov. 20, according to Yahoo Finance.

"Current period guidance was the best news of the day, coming in more than $3 billion ahead of the street's top line expectations," said Seeking Alpha analyst Bill Maurer.

Maurer added that the current valuation is much better than industry peers, even while the growth story is much more impressive.

"Nvidia's revenue growth largely stemmed from its data center business, where Nvidia benefits like no other company from the huge investments by Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon.com (AMZN), and many more. It feels like every week, we get a new announcement about a major data center from these or other tech companies, and Nvidia is providing the chips for most of these data centers – Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is only a minor competitor, at least for now. Nvidia's data center business contributed around 90% of Nvidia's overall revenues," said Seeking Alpha analyst Jonathan Weber.

However, not all analysts had bullish sentiments.

Nvidia's robust growth is fueled by surging demand for Blackwell GPUs from an expanding (somewhat circular) AI ecosystem, said Seeking Alpha analyst Ahan Vashi from The Quantamental Investor.

"Despite raising revenue forecasts and modeling for aggressive growth, NVDA's valuation implies a -17% downside and a 5-year CAGR return below TQI's investment hurdle rate," said Vashi. The analyst added that "I maintain a Hold rating on NVDA at $197, due to stretched valuation; however, I would consider buying if shares drop to the mid-$100s without business deterioration over the next year."

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analyst Kumquat Research rated Nvidia a Sell. The analyst said investors should take a cautious approach and potentially trim positions as competition in the GPU space heats up, and Nvidia's "valuation appears lofty."

"No monopoly with this much reach and that serves customers with pockets as deep as Microsoft, Meta, Apple, and more can last forever, and I think that reckoning is coming for Nvidia even if the AI bubble refuses to burst. As such, I am rating NVDA a Sell," Kumquat Research added.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

data.title: Annexon stock rises after director purchases shares
data.description:
• Annexon (ANNX) stock rose after the company's director disclosed a purchase of shares for an approximate value of about $5M.
  • The stock is up about 15% today.
  • Muneer Satter, director at Annexon, bought about 1.5M shares between a price range of $2.99 and $3.94, as disclosed in SEC filings.

data.title: Sell the rip: market gains evaporate as Nvidia does round trip
data.description:
The morning bull stampede has disappeared as sellers wiped out huge opening gains market gains Thursday.

The Nasdaq (COMP.IND) (QQQ) -0.1% has lost more than 2 percentage points as Nvidia's (NVDA) premarket gains of more than 5% disappeared. The S&P (SP500) (SPY) and Dow (DJI) (DIA) are also in the red. 

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) -3.5% telegraphed risk-off sentiment as it started to decline around 9 a.m. ET, about 2 hours before equities.

data.title: Design Therapeutics spikes as RBC upgrades on an eventful 2026
data.description:
Design Therapeutics (DSGN) added ~15% on Thursday after RBC Capital Markets upgraded the stock to Outperform from Sector Perform, citing a catalyst-rich period for the small molecule drug developer in the near-term.

The upgrade comes after the Carlsbad, California-based Design (DSGN) earlier this month projected an H2 2026 timeline to release data from its RESTORE-FA Phase 1/2 MAD trial for DT-216P2, an experimental therapy for the neurodegenerative disorder Friedreich's ataxia.

"We believe DSGN will have a more eventful 2026, and as we turn the calendar, we took the time to dive into the new DT-216p2 formulation for their lead FA program," analyst Leonid Timashev wrote, raising his price target on Design (DSGN) to $13 from $6 per share.

“By mapping 'P2's new exposure to mRNA and protein expression, we believe the drug can show clinically meaningful benefits and drive a significant stock inflection.”

Citing the near-term catalysts and a higher likelihood of success, the analyst projects room for appreciation and argues that "DESGN is not receiving sufficient credit” for a potential revenue opportunity worth more than $1.9B by 2034.

data.title: Cantaloupe falls amid report on FTC review of 365 Retail deal
data.description:
Cantaloupe Inc. (CTLP) dropped 1.3% after a report on the Federal Trade Commission's review of its planned sale to 365 Retail Markets.

The deal has drawn third party complaints about interoperability and bundling, according to traders, who cited a Capitol Forum report that was circulating on Thursday. The FTC's investigation, prior to the recent government shutdown, was heavily staffed on the deal.

The FTC's main concern with the combination is, will it reduce head-to-head competition in micro markets, according to the report. 

The report comes after Cantaloupe (CTLP) disclosed in September that it received a second request  from the FTC for its planned sale to 365 Retail Markets. The parties expect the deal to be completed in the first half of next year after originally forecasting a close in 2H 2025.

In June, Cantaloupe (NASDAQ:CTLP) agreed to be acquired by 365 Retail Markets, a provider of unattended retail technologies, in a transaction with an equity value of $848 million. 365 Retail agreed to pay $11.20 a share for Cantaloupe.

data.title: Accenture acquires RANGR data to boost Palantir talent and capabilities
data.description:
• Accenture (ACN) has acquired RANGR Data, a U.S.-based certified Palantir partner, to expand its engineering talent and strengthen its ability to deliver large-scale, AI-driven transformation projects.
  • RANGR, which specializes in customized data strategies, supply chain management, system integration and real-time analytics, brings a 40-person team with expertise in Palantir Foundry and AIP.
  • The deal is expected to accelerate the growth of Accenture’s Palantir business in the U.S.
  • The transaction is part of Accenture’s ongoing acquisitions focused on growing AI capabilities, following recent deals including Palantir consultancy Decho, Salesforce AI consultancy NeuraFlash and AI firm Halfspace.
  • ACN is -0.17% to $240.65.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: New Jersey Resources outlines $5B capital plan with 7%-9% EPS growth target through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: New Jersey Resources Corporation (NJR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen D. Westhoven opened by highlighting "excellent results in fiscal 2025, driven by strong execution and performance," noting that for the fifth consecutive year, NJR exceeded its initial earnings guidance and long-term growth targets. He emphasized consistency and execution, stating guidance for net financial earnings per share (NFEPS) of $3.03 to $3.18 for fiscal 2026, aligning with the company's long-term 7% to 9% growth rate and allowing for further upside. Significant capital deployment was detailed, with an expected investment of roughly $5 billion over the next 5 years, representing a 40% increase over the previous 5-year period, with about 60% allocated to New Jersey Natural Gas. Westhoven also stressed that "NJR requires no block equity issuance to execute on its capital plan" and highlighted a "healthy balance sheet anchored and disciplined financial management."

  • The CEO described high single-digit rate base growth at New Jersey Natural Gas through 2030, and projected that Storage & Transportation (S&T) is "expected to more than double net financial earnings by 2027," citing favorable recontracting. He noted a recent FERC filing to increase Leaf River's working gas capacity by over 70%. Clean Energy Ventures (CEV) expects to "expand capacity by more than 50% over the next 2 years" with a robust pipeline of projects.

  • CFO Roberto Bel stated, "Fiscal 2025 was an excellent year with strong even growth, a solid balance sheet and continued investment across our businesses." He highlighted that CEV placed 93 megawatts of new commercial solar capacity into service, raising the portfolio to 479 megawatts, and stressed the company's "30 consecutive years of dividend increases and reporting confidence in our long-term plan." Bel also reported that approximately $0.30 per share of performance came from the sale of the initial solar portfolio and detailed the company's record investments and solid winter results from Energy Services.

Outlook

  • Management initiated fiscal 2026 NFEPS guidance at $3.03 to $3.18 per share, consistent with the 7% to 9% long-term growth target. The company expects to invest $4.8 billion to $5.2 billion in capital through fiscal 2030, a 40% increase over the previous five years. More than 60% of projected CapEx will be dedicated to the utility, with the remainder allocated to CEV and S&T. Management expects the utility to contribute approximately 70% of fiscal 2026 NFEPS.

Financial Results

  • NJR finished the year at the top end of its previously raised guidance range, with roughly two-thirds of total EPS attributed to the utility segment. When excluding the net impact of the sale of residential solar assets, this figure rises to over 70%. CFO Roberto Bel highlighted the "completion of our rate case and a record year of saving investment" as performance drivers.

  • The company deployed $850 million in CapEx across its businesses in fiscal 2025. New Jersey Natural Gas represented 64% of total CapEx, focused on strengthening infrastructure and supporting customer growth. CEV CapEx exceeded expectations due to accelerated project progress, with a fully safe-harbored capital deployment to secure future tax benefits. The balance sheet remains strong, with ample liquidity and a well-laddered debt maturity profile.

Q&A

  • Gabriel Moreen, Mizuho: Asked about S&T contract renegotiations and the timeline for FID on expansions at Leaf River. Westhoven responded, "the contract tenure at Leaf River, they've got various terms...the average contract rate was probably about $0.09 a dekatherm per month. We're now up to almost $0.20 dekatherm per month on average. So big contract upgrade there." On expansions, he stated, "before we make any investment, we've got contracts to back it...we've got signed contracts in certain really quite a bit of clarity on where the revenues are coming to support those investments."

  • Moreen also inquired about CEV project construction and delays. Westhoven indicated construction cycles span fiscal years, with many shovel-ready projects and "a great number of options."

  • Jamieson Ward, Jefferies, questioned the deployment timeline for safe-harbored CEV projects and the earnings cadence. Westhoven pointed to the capital plan as the "most accurate picture of what we're going to be able to achieve," with flexibility to accelerate.

  • Ward also probed contract life and roll-off in CEV. Westhoven described the business as long-term, with opportunities to repower or add batteries to existing facilities, emphasizing organic growth.

  • Ward followed up on affordability efforts in New Jersey. Westhoven reiterated, "natural gas is the cheapest way that you can keep your home in business."

  • Elias Jossen, JPMorgan, asked about headwinds to the EPS growth outlook. Westhoven responded, "it's all positive at this point...confident in all those things."

  • Travis Miller, Morningstar, asked about S&T earnings drivers and recontracting impact. Westhoven stated, "for existing assets and no capital investment we've been able to double the earnings coming from those assets, and that's really driven by better contracts."

  • Miller also asked about M&A capacity. Westhoven said, "if something comes along and it happens to fit and fits organically, we would take a look at it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was constructive, focused on growth drivers, contract rollovers, and capital deployment timelines, with recurring questions on acceleration and earnings cadence.

  • Management maintained a confident, steady tone throughout, using phrases like "we feel well positioned going forward" and "confident in all those things." The sentiment was positive, with no defensive or hesitant language detected.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence remained high, with greater detail on contract upgrades and business segment outlooks, and analysts stayed focused on growth, but with more emphasis on execution timelines and capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NFEPS shifted from $3.20-$3.30 for 2025 (Q3) to $3.03-$3.18 for 2026, with both ranges aligned to the company’s 7%-9% long-term growth target.

  • CapEx projections were updated from a range of $1.3B-$1.6B (Q3) to $4.8B-$5.2B over 5 years, representing a 40% increase in planned investment.

  • Strategic focus increased on S&T contract upgrades and Leaf River expansion, while CEV's capacity growth outlook was made more explicit.

  • Analysts' questions became more granular on contract specifics and project timelines, while management showed consistency and increased visibility on growth plans and capital needs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited maintaining strong credit metrics and liquidity as ongoing priorities.

  • No major operational or regulatory risks were highlighted for the near term, with management emphasizing "no block equity needed" and reaffirming a "balanced debt maturity profile."

  • Analyst concerns centered on execution timing, contract rollovers, and maintaining affordability for customers in New Jersey.

Final Takeaway

New Jersey Resources delivered strong performance in fiscal 2025, exceeded initial earnings expectations for the fifth consecutive year, and set a clear path forward anchored by an ambitious $5 billion capital plan over five years. The company projects NFEPS of $3.03 to $3.18 for fiscal 2026, in line with its 7% to 9% growth target, and expects significant contributions from its utility and S&T segments, supported by contract upgrades and capacity expansions. Management expressed high confidence in its ability to execute on its growth and investment strategies while maintaining a solid balance sheet and long-term value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Unions at Woodside Energy's Pluto 2 LNG move closer to strike action
data.description:
A construction workers' union has applied to Australia's labor tribunal for permission to strike at Woodside Energy's (WDS) Pluto 2 project before year-end in a battle over pay at the plant that is under construction, Reuters reported Thursday.

A strike could slow work to expand Woodside's (WDS) Pluto LNG plant in Western Australia and delay the company's plan to ship the first liquefied natural gas cargo from Pluto 2 in H2 2026.

The union group said salary negotiations with building contractor Bechtel had gone nowhere and its next move was to strike, maintaining that workers at Pluto 2 are receiving an hourly rate that is 30% less than workers doing the same job at the Chevron-operated Wheatstone LNG project, when factoring in changes in the consumer price index.

Woodside (WDS) recently said Train 2, with a capacity of 5 million metric tons/year of LNG, was 91% complete and on track to send its first cargo in next year's H2.

data.title: Wall Street pares gains after Nvidia results and strong Sept jobs data
data.description:
U.S. stocks pare earlier gains on Thursday after earlier market pop as Nvidia’s (NVDA) results reset the market’s risk tone for the time being. Adding to the momentum, the long-awaited September jobs report finally landed, delivering payroll growth that easily topped expectations.

Leading the charge higher on the session was the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) as it was last up by 0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had pushed up by 0.4% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed by 0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, eight of the 11 S&P segments find themselves up in positive territory and are led by Consumer Staples. Going in the other direction, Industrials has been the weakest performer thus far.

As for stocks that were on the move, Nvidia was +0.5% after adding more than $200B to its market cap with its post-earnings rally, nearing the $200/share level it lost early this month.

Thursday's spotlight was on the September nonfarm payrolls report, which was initially supposed to come out on October 3. However, the longest U.S. government shutdown in history derailed that release.

The data showed the U.S. labor market added 119K jobs in September, easily outstripping the consensus of 50K. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in the month, a tad higher than the expected 4.3%.

“Thursday's jobs report was much stronger than expected and it's possible that the Federal Reserve may take more of a wait and see approach to rates in December, especially since there is still uncertainty over the economic data, which has only first started to come back online after the drought from the government shutdown. Thursday's jobs report suggests that the labor market is not as weak as feared,” Alexander Guiliano, chief investment officer, Resonate Wealth Partners stated.

Moreover, Treasury yields shifted lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 2 basis points to 4.11% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 2 basis points to 3.57%.
